lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly manufacturing motor homes on purchased chassis or conversion vans on an assembly-line basis for external dealers, distributors, rental fleets, and consumers.
lens.excluded_activities: Large integrated OEMs outside the EBITDA band; captive plants; one-off van customization and repair; towable travel trailers; mobile homes; chassis-only or complete body-and-chassis manufacturing classified elsewhere.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Wholesale or direct product sales of Type A, B, and C motor homes and production conversion vans, with dealer orders, model-year programs, options, parts, and warranty support creating repeat revenue relationships.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for the broader NAICS 336200 group, not 336213, and does not measure AI exposure. I map partial exposure to dealer quoting, configuration, design-document reuse, purchasing, production planning, marketing content, warranty triage, and quality records while applying little exposure to physical assembly and finishing. Motor homes can carry more consumer marketing and configuration work than the broader group, so the proxy may understate exposure, but extensive craft assembly creates downward bias risk in the other direction.
a.rationale: The broader group is 65.21% production occupations and 31.75% assemblers/fabricators. AI opportunity therefore lies mostly in indirect labor and selected inspection/documentation tasks rather than construction of the vehicle and living space.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted occupation roster and activity logs from motor-home manufacturers covering model engineering, dealer orders, scheduling, procurement, marketing, quality, and warranty versus physical production.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data include trailers and bodies.
Exposure does not equal feasible automation.

rho.rationale: Digital dealer/order workflows, repeat model families, extensive parts content, and warranty knowledge bases are implementable within five years. Fragmented systems, seasonal staffing, high product variation, data quality, safety certification, and the cost of integration constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Documented AI deployments across several independent RV manufacturers with adoption rates, labor hours, quote/order cycle time, warranty accuracy, rework, and system-integration cost over multiple model years.
caveats: No industry study directly measures five-year implementation.
Human certification and final quality accountability remain necessary.

e.rationale: Census reports only 71 employer establishments, and recognizable motor-home brands include sizable consolidated manufacturers. Some specialty Class B converters and niche brands may fit the band, but captive facilities, under-scale customizers, and businesses outside the margin or EBITDA band reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A current establishment-to-enterprise map for all 71 reported establishments with ultimate owner, revenue, normalized EBITDA, production versus custom-conversion status, dealer repeat rate, and captive flags.
caveats: Establishment count is not firm count.
The injected n of 21 is margin-bridged and may overstate independent firms in this concentrated niche.

s5.bridge: Census ABS owner-age tables cover employer businesses generally, not qualifying motor-home manufacturers or actual control transfers. I treat owner aging as succession pressure but discount for corporate ownership, internal succession, brand closures, recapitalizations without control, and failed sale processes; industry concentration likely makes the broad proxy overstate eligible transfers.
s5.rationale: Niche founders may face succession, but the eligible denominator is small and transactions are sensitive to the RV cycle, brand strength, dealer inventory, warranty liabilities, and buyer financing.
s5.change_evidence: Verified PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and RV-industry transactions over five years, tagged for control and matched to a firm-level eligible population.
caveats: Owner age does not observe sales.
Small private deals and closures are incompletely recorded.

q.rationale: Brand, floorplan differentiation, option mix, dealer allocation, lead time, and warranty performance permit meaningful retention. Dealer bargaining, model-year discounting, transparent MSRP/invoice comparisons, financing sensitivity, and price competition pass through a material share over renewals and product cycles.
q.change_evidence: Dealer agreements, incentive schedules, model-year pricing, option margins, floorplan support, and post-automation gross-margin bridges across comparable models.
caveats: Industry PPI is a selling-price measure, not an AI pass-through measure.
Direct-to-consumer and dealer-heavy brands have different retention.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve real production measures 336213 output and RVIA counts manufacturer wholesale shipments, but neither is a five-year constant-quality demand forecast for LMM lens firms. I combine a post-boom output trough with recent motor-home shipment recovery and broader outdoor-recreation activity; shipments can reflect dealer inventory changes and thus overstate end demand during restocking.
d5.rationale: Real production fell from 122.54 in 2022 to 65.87 in 2024, then recovered to 73.36 in 2025. RVIA reports 36,029 motor homes shipped in 2025, up 3.3%, and early 2026 motor-home shipments were up 17.7% year to date through February. BEA reports RVing generated $27.5 billion of 2024 value added. These signals support a near-flat-to-moderate recovery base with wide cyclical tails.
d5.change_evidence: Monthly Type A/B/C retail registrations, dealer inventory and turn rates, cancellation and discount data, lending approvals, used-RV prices, and five-year demographic demand forecasts.
caveats: Wholesale shipments can diverge from retail sell-through.
Outdoor-recreation value added is far broader than motor-home purchases.
Interest rates and consumer confidence drive large cycle swings.

o.rationale: A motor home is a physical highway vehicle and living-space product that must be assembled, integrated, safety-certified, warranted, and delivered by an accountable manufacturer. AI may alter design, sales, and administrative labor but cannot replace the operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that chassis OEMs, robotic contract manufacturers, or consumer self-build platforms fully displace independent branded manufacturers across material market segments.
caveats: Consolidation, imports, or dealer private labels can displace particular lens firms while the operator requirement remains.

narrative.executive_view: Motor-home manufacturing has useful digital workflows around configured products, dealer networks, marketing, and warranty, but remains a highly cyclical consumer-durable business with a small eligible firm pool and physically intensive production.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve dealer-order handling, floorplan and option retrieval, bills of material, engineering-change search, procurement, schedules, marketing content, owner-manual support, and warranty triage. Cabinetry, plumbing, electrical integration, upholstery, paint, assembly, road testing, and final inspection remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Brand and differentiated floorplans protect some gains, particularly in niche Class B/C products, while dealer leverage, incentives, product refreshes, and easy price comparison return gains to customers over time.
narrative.firm_availability: Only 71 employer establishments were reported, and establishment is not independent firm. The LMM opportunity is likely concentrated in specialty converters and niche brands, making ownership and revenue-quality verification essential.
narrative.demand_durability: The sector is recovering from a sharp post-pandemic production decline. Recent motor-home shipments and outdoor-recreation activity are supportive, but purchases remain discretionary and sensitive to financing, wealth, fuel prices, used inventory, and dealer stocking.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are consumer-durable cyclicality, interest rates, dealer inventory, brand concentration, warranty and recall exposure, chassis supply, skilled trades, seasonal labor, floorplan financing, consolidation, and overestimating the count of transferable independent manufacturers.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat model families and dealer relationships create reusable information workflows alongside a recovering shipment base.
narrative.principal_weakness: A tiny, concentrated eligible pool and discretionary demand make availability and durability fragile.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 336213 Motor Home Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines motor homes on purchased chassis and assembly-line conversion vans and distinguishes excluded chassis, custom conversion, towable, and mobile-home activities.

sources.title: 336213 Motor Home Manufacturing — Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Reports 71 employer establishments in 2023 CBP and provides the six-digit industry definition.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Motor Vehicle Body and Trailer Manufacturing
sources.supports: Broader occupation mix: production occupations 65.21% and assemblers/fabricators 31.75%.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Motor Home Manufacturing, annual
sources.supports: Real-output index values of 122.5407 in 2022, 87.3771 in 2023, 65.8724 in 2024, and 73.3577 in 2025.

sources.title: Producer Price Index: Motor Home Manufacturing
sources.supports: BLS monthly industry selling-price index, including 230.211 in May 2026.

sources.title: RV shipments end 2025 with modest growth
sources.supports: RVIA manufacturer survey reports 36,029 motor-home shipments in 2025, up 3.3%, within 342,220 total RV shipments.

sources.title: Outdoor Recreation Economic Statistics, 2024
sources.supports: BEA reports RVing was the second-largest conventional outdoor activity with $27.5 billion in current-dollar value added in 2024.

sources.title: ABS Characteristics of Business Owners: 2024 Tables
sources.supports: Official Census Age of Owner tables for employer businesses, used only as a broad succession proxy.

sources.title: NHTSA interpretation: final-stage manufacturer responsibilities
sources.supports: Final-stage manufacturers complete vehicles under federal safety standards and affix certification labels under 49 CFR Parts 567 and 568.
